questions.question: What is the standardized DSCP value for Expedited Forwarding (voice traffic) and what is its binary form?
questions.explanation: EF (Expedited Forwarding, RFC 3246) is the voice PHB. The binary codepoint 101110 (= decimal 46) is what the IP ToS byte carries on every voice packet, after the iptables `-j DSCP --set-dscp 46` mark.

questions.question: Why does LLQ (Low-Latency Queuing) cap the EF priority queue at ~33% of the link bandwidth?
options: A. Because the EF DSCP only allows 33% of the bits
B. Without a ceiling, the priority queue starves all other classes -- the ceil ensures EF gets bounded low-latency service without monopolizing the link
questions.explanation: A naive `prio` queue drains band 0 before everything else. Without an `htb ceil` rate limit on the EF class, voice could push 100% of the link and starve video/bulk/management. RFC 8628 recommends policing EF to a fixed percentage (33% is the Cisco/IETF default).

questions.question: What does the `tc filter ... match ip tos 0xb8 0xfc flowid 1:1` line actually do?
options: A. It matches all packets with ToS byte 0xb8 (DSCP EF) and routes them into queue 1:1 -- the priority band; the mask 0xfc ignores the 2 CU/ECN bits
B. It blocks all DSCP EF traffic
C. It sets the ToS byte to 0xb8 on every packet
D. It re-marks DSCP EF to DSCP BE
questions.explanation: `0xb8` = 10111000 binary = DSCP 46 in the high 6 bits. The mask `0xfc` = 11111100 ignores the low 2 bits (CU/ECN). This filter sends every EF-marked packet to band 0 of the `prio` qdisc -- the priority queue. This is how the LLQ selects voice traffic from the bulk flow.
